subsection.title: Limits
subsection.p: The value f(a) does not have to exist.

subsection.title: Common limit laws
subsection.p: If the limits exist, then \lim_{x\to a}(f+g)=\lim_{x\to a}f+\lim_{x\to a}g, \lim_{x\to a}(fg)=\left(\lim_{x\to a}f\right)\left(\lim_{x\to a}g\right), and, if \lim_{x\to a}g(x)\ne0, \lim_{x\to a}\frac{f}{g}=\frac{\lim_{x\to a}f}{\lim_{x\to a}g}.

subsection.title: Continuity
subsection.p: A function f is continuous at a if \boxed{\lim_{x\to a}f(x)=f(a).}
This requires three things: f(a)\text{ is defined}, \lim_{x\to a}f(x)\text{ exists}, and \lim_{x\to a}f(x)=f(a).

subsection.title: Common continuous functions
subsection.p: Rational functions are continuous where their denominators are nonzero.
The functions \sin x,\qquad \cos x,\qquad e^x are continuous everywhere.
The function \ln x is continuous for x\gt0.
Compositions of continuous functions are continuous where they are defined.

statement.p: If f is continuous on [a,b], and N lies between f(a) and f(b), then there is some c\in[a,b] such that f(c)=N.
If f is continuous on a closed interval [a,b], then f has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on [a,b].

subsection.title: Warnings
subsection.p: A limit can exist even if f(a) is missing.
A function can be defined at a but still fail to be continuous there.
Continuity on an open interval does not guarantee an absolute maximum or minimum.
In several variables, checking only one path is not enough to prove a limit exists.
